coredata.dc:title: Cryogenic properties of copper produced by Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Walcz, Erik
Budai, Csaba
coredata.dc:description: Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM) represents cutting-edge technology in the field of cost-effective additive manufacturing, while the vacuum tightness and thermal properties of these materials are not fully measured, especially at low, cryogenic temperatures. This study investigates the viability of utilizing ADAM for producing components intended for cryogenic heat transfer in vacuum. In this paper I will present the CryPT-ON (Cryogenic Performance Test) cryostat at the Fusion Plasma Physics Department of HUN-REN Centre for Energy Research, the specific experimental setup for thermal conductivity, diffusivity measurement and the 3D printed samples with X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) measurement to determine the material composition and have a better understanding of this special material and printing technology.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Hungary institutions: Hybrid, Full OA RAP 2025

SEM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The CAD model of the CryPT-ON cryostat.
3D printed copper samples with support structures.
X-ray fluorescence spectrum of the 3D printed sample chamber.
The cross section of the CAD model with measurement and heating points.
Measured thermal conductivity between 14 K and 54 K.
Temperature-dependent thermal conductivity of copper with different purities. [11].
Induced thermal waves and their phase shift at the two ends of the specimen.
Measured thermal diffusivity on different temperatures and a hypothetical shape of diffusivity curve. (if we assume it is copper-like).
Calculated specific heat from thermal conductivity, diffusivity and density.
Microscopic image of the sample piece and a cast OFHC copper.
Microscopic image of the sample piece and a cast OFHC copper after etching. (For interpretation of the colors in the figure(s), the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM image of the sample piece and a cast OFHC copper after etching.
Markforged Copper composition as per datasheet [7].

head.ce:title: Cryogenic properties of copper produced by Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fusion Plasma Physics Department, Institute for Atomic Energy Research, HUN-REN Centre for Energy Research, Budapest, Hungary
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fusion Plasma Physics Department, Institute for Atomic Energy Research, HUN-REN Centre for Energy Research, Konkoly-Thege Miklós út 29-33., H-1121 Budapest, Hungary

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechatronics, Optics and Mechanical Engineering Informatics, Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Budapest University of Technology and Economics, Budapest, Hungary

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechatronics, Optics and Mechanical Engineering Informatics
Budapest University of Technology and Economics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechatronics, Optics and Mechanical Engineering Informatics, Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Budapest University of Technology and Economics, Műegyetem rkp. 3., H-1111 Budapest, Hungary

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM) represents cutting-edge technology in the field of cost-effective additive manufacturing, while the vacuum tightness and thermal properties of these materials are not fully measured, especially at low, cryogenic temperatures. This study investigates the viability of utilizing ADAM for producing components intended for cryogenic heat transfer in vacuum. In this paper I will present the CryPT-ON (Cryogenic Performance Test) cryostat at the Fusion Plasma Physics Department of HUN-REN Centre for Energy Research, the specific experimental setup for thermal conductivity, diffusivity measurement and the 3D printed samples with X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) measurement to determine the material composition and have a better understanding of this special material and printing technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM) represents a cutting-edge technology in the realm of cost-effective additive manufacturing that leverages advanced techniques to fabricate complex, high-performance components. ADAM utilizes atomic diffusion processes, in which powdered metals are bound together layer by layer through a combination of thermal sintering and the removal of binders [1]. Compared to other additive manufacturing technologies, the ADAM technology has the major advantage that the support structures can be manually removed after printing without requiring a subsequent machining process. This is because a Ca interlayer (based on our XRF measurements) is printed between the two surfaces, which can be snapped off. We have no knowledge of any previous in-depth investigations into the cryogenic applications of 3D printed materials, so as part of a measurement program, we are first examining the ADAM technology. However, despite its numerous advantages, concerns have been raised regarding the performance and reliability of ADAM-produced parts, particularly when subjected to extreme environmental conditions, such as cryogenic temperatures and the need for vacuum tightness.

One of the primary concerns is the potential for imperfections in the bonding of metal powders after thermal sintering, which may result in structural defects or voids that could compromise the mechanical and thermal properties of the final part. Such imperfections may become critical in applications that require high thermal conductivity and diffusivity, especially under cryogenic conditions, where material performance is significantly altered.

To investigate these concerns, an experimental evaluation was conducted using a new experimental device called CryPT-ON to assess the behavior of materials at cryogenic temperatures [2]. This evaluation focused on the heat transfer performance of ADAM-produced parts, with a particular emphasis on their thermal conductivity and diffusivity by measuring the propagation of thermal waves through the material. This experimental approach allowed for a detailed analysis of the thermal properties of the parts, offering valuable data on their suitability for use in cryogenic environments [3].

New experimental infrastructure has been built for nuclear fusion related cryogenic and vacuum experiments at HUN-REN Centre for Energy Research based on previous experiences with Shattered Pellet Injectors (SPI) developed for ITER Disruption Mitigation Systems [4] [5] [6]. The infrastructure (called CryPT-ON) consists of a rectangular vacuum chamber with interchangeable lids (shown on Fig. 1 ), MLI insulated radiation shileds, a vacuum pumping system,
H
2
, N and Ne gas infrastructure and He, N collector and flow control system. The cooling of the shield is possible with the 1st stage of the SHI RDE-418D4 cryocooler or with the integrated LN pipes on the shield. The chamber is also capable to handle a self-made Continuous Flow Cryostat (CFC) where the shield cooling is also possible with the evaporated outgoing Helium.

The primary objectives include conducting material testing under low-temperature conditions to assess material properties, advancing the production of solid hydrogen (protium) through desublimation to support fueling in fusion devices, validating computational models that simulate the desublimation process, and developing technologies for producing and injecting cryogenic pellets continuously. Another research field is to develop efficient hydrogen orto-para catalysis for nuclear fusion-related cryogenic pellets, due to the more favourable thermal conductivity properties.

As shown on Fig. 2 , two samples were printed, one for the thermal conductivity and diffusivity measurements and one for leak tightness measurements. According to the datasheet, the material has a relatively low percentage of pure copper compared to the standard ones and contains alloys such as iron. The thermal conductivity is given at room temperature; its value is 350
W
m ⋅ K
, but is calculated from diffusivity assuming that the specific heat is 0.385
J
g ⋅ K
. The relative density of the sintered material is 98% compared to the theoretical value of 8.96 g / cc
[7].

The sample is connected to a simple E-Cu copper block on each side with bolts and spring washer; sufficient thermal contacts were provided by Indium foil between them. Each block contains a temperature diode (Lakeshore DT-670) and a heater cartridge. The rectangular blocks on both ends of the specimen also contains their own temperature diode, these are calibrated ones and installed to the edge of the prismatic part. The measurement wires are anchored to the measured surfaces with bobbins and varnish. The upper block is attached to the second stage of the cryocoolers cold head with Apiezon grease. Additional inner shield was installed on the top of the 2nd stage of the cryocooler to minimise the radiation on the sample, because normally the main shield is on 50 K without additional load [8] [9] [10].

Fig. 4 shows the experimental setup in its installed state (on the left side of the image, a temporary brace is visible, which was used for the safety of the specimen and was removed after installation). Fig. 5 shows the CAD drawing of the assembly in cross-section. The temperature sensors are labeled ‘T0’, among which T06 and T07 are calibrated (with an accuracy of ±12 mK and ±20 mK within the measurement range used in the experiment). The ‘H’ labels indicate the locations of the heating cartridges, which are Lakeshore HTR-25-100 models with a power output of 100 W. During the thermal conductivity measurement, the power of the H1 and H3 heating cartridges was used to set the initial temperature of the sample, and the thermal conductivity of the sample was determined based on the temperature change in T07 due to the known power applied to the H2 heating cartridge, calculated relative to the average temperature of T06 and T07.

If a long, thin bar of material undergoes periodic heating, a temperature wave travels along the sample, with its propagation properties revealing the material's thermal diffusivity. As the wave moves further along the bar, it becomes significantly attenuated and experiences a phase shift. The behavior of this thermal wave is closely linked to the thermal diffusivity of the material. Thus, thermal diffusivity can be determined both by measuring the phase lag and the reduction in amplitude of the thermal wave, as shown by the following equations (1) and (2). [12] [13]
(1)
Δ ϕ = x
ω
2 α
(2)
u
0
u
x
=
e
x
ω
2 α
where Δϕ is the phase shift, x is the distance between the measurement points, α is the diffusivity, ω is the angular frequency,
u
0
is the amplitude at point 0, and
u
x
is the amplitude at point x.

The physical setup of the system is the same as previously presented and shown in Fig. 5. The temperature of the specimen is controlled by the H3 heating cartridge, while the H1 heating cartridge is periodically operated via a solid-state relay, set at a specific voltage level on a laboratory power supply. The propagation of thermal waves is recorded over time by the T06 and T07 sensors, reaching the T06 measurement point first, followed by T07.

On Fig. 8 , the phase shift of the thermal waves induced in the sample at approximately 43 K can be clearly observed, and the significant amplitude difference is also evident from the extreme values of the two vertical axes. In this case, the heating cartridge was driven at a frequency of 0.005 Hz with a power of 3.7 W and a 50% duty cycle, resulting in a measured phase shift of 1.68 rad. Using the known length of the sample (180 mm) and applying Eq. (1), we obtain a diffusivity value of 2.951 ×
10
− 4
m
2
/ s .

By performing the experiment at two additional temperatures, we obtain the points shown in Fig. 10. When comparing these points with the curves for copper found in the literature (Fig. 9
), and assuming that, similar to the thermal conductivity, the diffusivity exhibits a similar characteristic trend, we can draw a hypothetical curve for illustrative purposes. From the measurement points, we can observe that the diffusivity of our sample falls significantly below that of copper with a known RRR = 30. However, this is not surprising, as after the thermal conductivity measurement, we had already established that, in terms of purity, it likely falls between RRR = 10 and 20.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density and specific heat capacity calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
The relationship between thermal diffusivity (α), thermal conductivity (k), density (ρ), and specific heat capacity (
c
p
) is fundamental. Equation (3) links the material's ability to conduct heat with its capacity to store it, determining the rate of heat transfer through the material.
(3)
α =
k
ρ
c
p

Based on the datasheet of the material [7] the foreseen density is 98% of the 8 , 960
kg/m
3
nominal density. After printing, we determined the mass and volume of the sample, which yielded a density of 8 , 601 ± 82
kg/m
3
(assuming 100% infill). By considering the density as constant, we can calculate the specific heat for a few previous measurement points using the diffusivity and thermal conductivity as it is shown on Fig. 11 .

As presented in the previous chapter, the other 3D printed sample was designed to examine the vacuum application and vacuum tightness of parts made using this technology. After removing the printed chamber from the support, a 1/4-inch stainless steel tube was brazed onto the top of the sample chamber using silver brazing, and the entire assembly was placed in a vacuum chamber. The stainless steel tube was then connected to a vacuum feedthrough. After evacuating both the vacuum chamber and the sample chamber, a mass spectrometer was connected to the vacuum chamber, and the sample chamber was filled with helium at 1 atm through the vacuum feedthrough. The wall thickness of the sample chamber is 4 mm, and its internal surface area is 3,175 mm2. The mass spectrometer showed a significant increase in leak rate, jumping from an initial value of 6.4 ×
10
− 13
Pa ⋅ m3/s to 4.1 ×
10
− 6
Pa ⋅ m3/s after the sample chamber was filled. Therefore, we can conclude that vacuum applications for parts produced using this technology are not feasible without additional technological processes, such as copper plating.

To obtain information about the extent of porosity or imperfections in fusion, inferred from leaks and lower density compared to solid material, we prepared a cross-section from the sample. The sample was polished and examined under a microscope alongside an ordinary cast OFHC copper sample prepared with the same procedure, as shown in Fig. 12 . Comparing the two samples, we can conclude that no differences are visible on a micrometer scale between the two technologies; thus, any differences must be submicron-scale. This is not surprising since the maximum helium leakage observed, although not negligible from a vacuum technology perspective, does not represent a significant hole given the specific pressure difference, especially when considering the leakage distributed over the entire surface of the chamber.

After etching these same samples with iron(II)-chloride for 55 seconds, additional microscopic images were taken, shown in Fig. 13 . We can conclude that even in this case, no inclusions that could cause leakage are observable; however, there is a clear difference in grain structure between the two samples. In the case of the ADAM sample, the surface grain structure is more pronounced, with notably varied patterns and heterogeneous grain morphology. In contrast, the OFHC sample exhibits a finer surface grain structure with smaller, more uniform grains. Although the larger grain size observed in the ADAM sample could theoretically favor thermal conductivity, the heterogeneity arising from its layered structure, oxidation (crystals with different colors), and local contaminations produce an opposite effect.

Further investigations were carried out to obtain a more accurate understanding of our component produced using the ADAM technique. Therefore, we examined the etched surface using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). Fig. 14 shows SEM images of both etched ADAM and OFHC copper samples for comparison. It is clearly observable that, in the case of ADAM, significantly more distinct sub-grains formed, separated by deep, sharp grooves, while the OFHC sample exhibits fewer separate sub-grains with much more rounded boundaries.

We believe the diffusion heating process prevents the persistence of large, open pores. Instead, at most, smaller micropores, microcracks, and minor defects may remain, which are more difficult to identify even under SEM examination, and exhibit an irregular, non-uniform structure. Moreover, these defects might only be present in certain regions of the component (arising from geometry-dependent characteristics of the printing technology), whereas we have only examined a single cross-section.

This study has demonstrated the potential of Atomic Diffusion Additive Manufacturing (ADAM) for producing copper components for cryogenic applications while acknowledging its current limitations. Thermal conductivity and diffusivity measurements revealed that the performance of ADAM-produced copper parts at cryogenic temperatures is promising but constrained by the material's lower purity compared to traditional copper standards. The measured thermal diffusivity and conductivity suggest a purity level between RRR = 10 and RRR = 20, which may limit its use in certain cryogenic applications. Furthermore, vacuum tightness tests indicated that the current state of ADAM technology requires additional post-processing, such as copper plating, to achieve the structural integrity and leak tightness necessary for vacuum-related uses. In examining the lower thermal conductivity and the lack of vacuum tightness, we performed XRF measurements to determine the material composition, as well as microscopic and SEM analyses to investigate the structure. Based on the significant differences in thermal conductivity observed in OFHC copper with various RRR values, we concluded that although our ADAM-produced component has a lower density compared to solid copper, the considerable amount and variety of contaminants likely have a greater impact on the low-temperature thermal conductivity. These findings underscore the need for further development of the ADAM process to enhance its applicability for demanding cryogenic environments. During the investigations, we were the first to utilize the previously developed measurement technique based on the propagation of thermal waves for the cryogenic temperature characterization of 3D-printed components. This method is intended to be applied in the future to study the material properties of cryogenic pellets relevant to fusion experiments. Additionally, there is still no adequate literature on the applicability of other potential copper 3D printing technologies (e.g., SLS), which remain highly needed in cryogenic technology.
Erik Walcz: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Csaba Budai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Special thanks to H-ION Ltd. and Metalloexpert Ltd. for providing the 3D printed samples used in this study, to Dr. Imre Szalóki at HUN-REN CER SBL for performing the XRF measurements, to Levente Illés at HUN-REN CER NÉL for SEM images and to Dr. Sándor Zoletnik for his valuable professional support. Their contribution was essential to the realization of this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal wave analysis for measurements of thermal diffusivity
